## QUICK TAKEAWAY

You build systems that protect people when the stakes are high. Your career shows a consistent pattern of stepping into environments where operational infrastructure does not exist yet, building it, and holding the line on standards when pressure mounts to move faster. The mathematical precision your colleagues name is real, but what drives the work is something deeper: you manage downward better than upward because you see your team as people first, and you refuse to let institutional failure become their personal failure. The sports volunteer work, the Army service, the reputation for integrity under stress—all point to the same operational conviction: when things go wrong, the people who depend on the system should not be the ones who pay the price.

---

## WHAT ENERGIZES YOU

**You care about protecting people when institutions fail them.**

Your strongest reputation signal is the Wall Street bankruptcy story: 17 people on your team, the firm collapsing around them, and you made sure every single one of them landed safely. You did not just manage the transition; you took personal responsibility for their outcomes. That same orientation shows up in your alternative investments work: you built operational risk functions, chaired valuation committees, held veto authority over investments that failed operational standards. The work is about investor protection, but the energy comes from the same place: making sure the people who trust the system do not get hurt when the system breaks. Your passion for family and your volunteer work with your children's sports activities reinforce this: you show up for people who depend on you, and you do it consistently over time. If there is a second passion the work history suggests but you have not named explicitly, it is this: you care about fairness in environments where fairness is not structurally guaranteed. The governance roles you have chosen, the compliance authority you have held, the operational discipline you have built—all of it protects the people downstream from decisions made upstream.

---

## HOW YOU SHOW UP

**You lead through structure, not charisma.**

Your Affintus profile shows a preference for working alone and keeping to yourself, yet your career is full of leadership roles: managing teams, chairing committees, holding veto authority. The way you reconcile this is through systems: you build the infrastructure that makes the right decision the easy decision, so the team does not have to rely on your presence in the room. Your reputation for managing downward better than upward makes sense in this light: you care about the people who report to you because you see your job as building the conditions for their success, not managing up to secure your own position. You are straightforward and prefer integrity even when it costs you, which your colleagues experience as reliability: they know where you stand, and they know you will not shift when the pressure changes. The Army service and the "high integrity, well grounded" reputation note reinforce this: people trust you in high-stakes situations because you do not change your standards based on who is watching. You are not the person who works the room or sells the vision. You are the person who builds the system that keeps the room from collapsing.

---

## WHAT FUELS YOUR DRIVE

**You sustain effort when the people depending on you cannot protect themselves.**

The bankruptcy story is the clearest signal: the firm was failing, your team was vulnerable, and you worked through the collapse to make sure all 17 people landed safely. That is not just work ethic; that is a specific kind of fuel. You grind through complexity and ambiguity when the alternative is letting people down. Your career shows sustained intensity in high-stakes environments: scaling platforms to $10 billion AUM, building ORDD functions that held veto authority, directing compliance during acquisition readiness. The common thread is accountability: you hold yourself responsible for outcomes that other people depend on, and you do not stop until the outcome is secure. Your reputation for career and life guidance reinforces this: people call you when they need help navigating uncertainty, because they know you will stay with them until they are through it. The work ethic is not about proving yourself or chasing the next title. The work ethic is about finishing what you start when the people who trusted you to start it are counting on you to finish it.

---

## WHERE YOU'RE GROWING

**You keep learning how to build infrastructure in contexts where it does not exist yet.**

Your career arc shows consistent curiosity about new operational challenges: you moved from hedge funds to private equity to venture capital to BDCs, learning the operational nuances of each asset class. You built ORDD functions, valuation committees, compliance programs, and accelerator deal flow processes—each one a different kind of infrastructure problem. Your Affintus profile shows high openness to experience and a preference for creating solutions rather than following procedures, which maps directly to the pattern: you are drawn to environments where the playbook does not exist yet. The runway ahead is not about moving up a level; it is about expanding the scope of what you can build. You could scale to larger platforms (multi-billion AUM funds, institutional asset managers, global fund administrators), or you could go deeper into emerging contexts where the infrastructure is even less mature (family offices, fintech, digital assets, emerging managers). The scalability is in the breadth of contexts where you can apply the same operational discipline, not in the title progression within a single context. The potential is highest in environments where the organization knows it needs governance infrastructure but does not know how to build it, because that is where your capability is rarest.

---

## THE ENVIRONMENT THAT FITS YOU

You thrive in environments where the operational function does not exist yet, the stakes are high, and the people downstream are counting on you to get it right. You need autonomy to build the infrastructure the way you see it, without constant upward negotiation. You need decision authority that matches the accountability: if you are responsible for protecting investors or managing a team through a crisis, you need the authority to make the calls that protect them. You do not need a large platform or a well-known brand; you need a problem that matters and the freedom to solve it your way. You are comfortable in smaller organizations where you can own the outcome, and you are comfortable in high-ambiguity environments where the rules are not yet written. You do not thrive in environments where the infrastructure is already mature and the job is maintaining what someone else built, because that is not where your curiosity lives. You need work that has consequences: if you do it well, people are protected; if you do it poorly, people are harmed. That is the environment where your drive and your discipline both make sense.

The work you have done (building governance infrastructure, protecting investors, managing teams through crises) aligns with your values (independence, influence, family), but the industry context (alternative investments) may be incidental rather than intrinsic. If the industry itself matters to you beyond the operational challenges it provides, that strengthens the Industry Insider path. If the industry is context rather than passion, the Work That Matters path may be where the realignment lives. If this reading misses how you experience the industry, the feedback box below will help us adjust.

The alignment picture suggests the path forward is either amplification (same function, same or adjacent industry, contexts where you can build governance infrastructure at scale) or realignment (same operational capability, different mission context where the work connects more directly to what you care about beyond the job itself). The three paths below map both directions.

Leadership   See detail  How a person prefers to influence, inspire, and direct others to achieve shared goals  You prefer work that allows you to take charge of groups and you often try to include others at work. You enjoy planning the activities of a group and usually offer to lead such efforts. You are usually confident in your abilities to lead and have a good opinion of yourself.  Openness To Experience   See detail  The degree to which a person prefers work that is either predictable or variable  You prefer a job that constantly requires you to rely on your ability to create solutions. Organization   See detail  The degree of order and neatness a person prefers in a work space  You are able to tolerate some disorder in your surroundings but there are some things that you like to keep in order. Sociableness   See detail  The preference to be outgoing and extroverted, or to be reserved and introverted  You prefer work that allows you work alone and keep to yourself. Your preference is to work on your own and to share information as needed. You do not enjoy situations that bring attention to you or that put you at the center of a group.  Stamina   See detail  How a person approaches work, the work pace and how the direct their energy  You prefer a work pace that varies between slow and steady and very quick-paced. You believe that the truth is the best policy even when it may result in some consequences. Halverson                                        (555) 588-9300 cell • martin.halverson@gmail.com  •  LinkedIn Profile

Chief Financial Officer | Chief Operating Officer | Chief Compliance Officer

Alternative Investments • Fund Operations • Regulatory Compliance • Strategic Leadership

Senior financial executive with 20+ years of leadership across alternative investments, fund administration, and regulatory compliance, spanning hedge funds, private equity, venture capital, and business development companies (BDCs). Drove operational transformation at BioR2 Capital, overseeing a $1.3 billion AUM biotechnology fund with full accountability for finance, accounting, and service-provider relationships. Scaled Newburn Berman Alternative Investment Management to a ~$10 billion AUM platform by rebuilding the operational risk due diligence (ORDD) function and establishing the firm's first alternatives valuation committee. Recognized for delivering strategic results that attract institutional capital, support regulatory scrutiny, and position organizations for high-value outcomes, including acquisition by Sterling Sachs.

Expertise

Fund Governance & Compliance: SEC Registered Investment Advisor (RIA) Compliance – Operational Risk Due Diligence – Regulatory Reporting – Valuation Committee Oversight – Policy Development

Financial Operations & Reporting: Fund Accounting & Administration – GAAP & IFRS Reporting – Performance Reporting – Shadow Accounting – Daily Trade Reconciliation – LP Reporting & Capital Calls

Investment Due Diligence: Manager Selection & Vetting – Operational Risk Assessment – Portfolio Monitoring – Multi-Strategy Allocation – Alternative Asset Class Coverage

Strategic Leadership & Transformation: Organizational Build-Out – Cross-Functional Team Leadership – M&A Readiness – Executive Stakeholder Management – Succession Planning

Investor & Partner Relations: Institutional Investor Communication – Foundation & Endowment Advisory – ESG & Impact Investing – Consortium Partner Alignment – LP Relations – Deal Structuring

Innovation & Accelerator Management: Startup Evaluation & Cohort Management – Sports Technology Ecosystem Development – Strategic Investment Negotiation – Corporate Innovation Alignment

Revenue Growth & Business Transformation Achievements

Positioned firm for high-value acquisition by Sterling Sachs, leading operational improvements and compliance infrastructure build-out as COO/CCO of an impact investment firm managing over $500 million in assets under advisement for 11 of the top 25 foundations in the world.

Scaled alternative investment platform to ~$10 billion AUM at Newburn Berman by rebuilding the operational risk due diligence team to 17 professionals, instituting veto authority over non-compliant fund investments, and establishing the firm's first alternatives valuation committee.

Delivered oversight of $1.3 billion AUM biotechnology hedge fund and venture capital fund as outsourced CFO, implementing a new portfolio management system that streamlined daily trade allocations, reconciliations, and performance reporting.

Managed fund administration services for a platform with over $100 billion in assets under administration (AUA) at Cypress Financial Services, personally overseeing the firm's largest client by both assets and revenue.

Executive Career Progression

Boomspark Accelerators / Compass Universal SportsTech – Remote                                                                       2020 – Present

Managing Director

Strengthened the program's deal pipeline by leading a Deal Flow team that evaluates over 1,500 applicants annually and conducts 200+ interviews, selecting a final cohort of 10 companies per cycle.

Accelerated 5 successful cohorts (50 companies), generating a combined portfolio value of over $10 million across cohort companies.

Maximized partner alignment by collaborating closely with internal consortium stakeholders, including NU Sports, Compass Business, and Skylight Sports, resulting in over 750 pilot programs or partnerships launched between cohort companies and consortium partners.

Extended program impact through strategic engagement with 7 external consortium partners, including the Continental Football League, Pro Golf Tour, AutoRacing Circuit, Pro Wrestling Federation, American Aquatics, American Cycling, and US Snowsports, aligning cohort companies to partner innovation needs.

Protected program investment by structuring and negotiating stock purchase agreements with each of 50 selected cohort companies, representing over $10 million in total equity value on $2.5 million committed across all cohorts.



Vector Fund Solutions – Chicago, IL                                                                                                                                                 2016 – 2020

Chief Financial Officer (Outsourced) – BioR2 Capital, LLC

Elevated fund operational efficiency by managing all aspects of operations, finance, and accounting for a $1.3 billion AUM biotechnology hedge fund and venture capital fund spanning 6 funds.



Vector Fund Solutions (continued)

Improved portfolio oversight and reporting accuracy by instituting a new portfolio management system for daily trade allocations between funds, GAAP-compliant daily reconciliations, and performance reporting, reducing reconciliation cycle time by 30%.

Enhanced investor transparency by producing weekly and monthly performance estimates, inclusive of risk metrics for over 100 LPs, supporting LP reporting obligations and capital call administration.

Optimized vendor performance by managing 8 service provider relationships, including multiple prime brokers, fund administrator, auditor, and tax advisor, ensuring accountability and cost efficiency.

Supported management company scalability by directing all management company operations, accounting, and tax compliance, freeing investment leadership to focus exclusively on portfolio management.



Cypress Financial Services – Chicago, IL                                                                                                                                        2014 – 2016

Managing Director, Fund Administration

Drove operational excellence across a team of 15 professionals, overseeing fund accounting, investor relations and communication, compliance, and treasury functions for 75 client funds across hedge funds, fund of funds, and private equity strategies.

Generated firm revenue by personally managing the company's largest client relationships, representing approximately $10 billion in AUA, strengthening retention and service delivery standards.

Shaped firm strategy as a member of the Management Committee, driving numerous compliance policies and strategic initiatives that strengthened operational standards and competitive positioning across the platform.

Retained top-tier client relationships by delivering integrated fund administration across fund accounting, investor relations, compliance, and treasury, maintaining zero client attrition within the portfolio.



Mercer Impact Advisors, LLC – San Francisco, CA                                                                                                                      2012 – 2014

Chief Operating Officer / Chief Compliance Officer

Positioned the firm for acquisition by Sterling Sachs within 18 months by leading internal operational improvements across both internal and investment processes or functions, increasing infrastructure maturity, scalability, and regulatory compliance across ESG and impact investing operations.

Maintained regulatory standing by directing all SEC RIA compliance functions, including policies, procedures, filings, and examinations, for a firm advising 11 of the top 25 foundations in the world on environmental, social, and governance (ESG) and impact investing portfolios.

Delivered executive leadership across operations and compliance as COO/CCO, ensuring GAAP-compliant reporting and alignment between strategic goals and day-to-day fund operations for over $500 million in assets under advisement.



Continental Asset Management Limited – New York, NY                                                                                                      2009 – 2011

Global Head of Operational Risk Due Diligence

Protected investor capital by supervising ORDD coverage of 80+ underlying hedge fund investments with approximately $3 billion invested, ensuring portfolio-level operational standards.

Enforced institutional governance as a member of the Risk and Compliance Committee, reviewing over 30 investment decisions annually and setting portfolio guidelines aligned with client and internal policies.

Built the New York office into a fully operational parallel hub to the London headquarters, establishing local ORDD coverage, hiring 5 professionals, and reducing operational dependency on the London team.



Newburn Berman (formerly Lyndhurst) Alternative Investment Management – New York, NY 	                                     2006 – 2009

Senior Vice President, Global Head of Operational Risk Due Diligence, Hedge Fund Accounting and Administration

Delivered comprehensive platform oversight by supervising a staff of 17 supporting 10 hedge fund of funds portfolios (domestic and foreign), 5 single-manager hedge funds, and minority stakes in several hedge fund managers, with AUM reaching approximately $10 billion at peak.

Mitigated investment risk by overseeing operational risk for over 100 underlying hedge fund investments, with formal veto authority over any new hedge fund investment failing to meet operational risk standards.

Strengthened risk governance by restructuring the ORDD team from 1 to 4 professionals and implementing policies that established clear operational standards for all underlying hedge fund managers.

Accelerated operational readiness by building two teams from scratch: a shareholder services team and a fund accounting team that collectively assumed full GAAP-compliant accounting coverage for over 50 funds across fund of funds and single-strategy mandates.

Improved administrator accountability by constructing shadow accounting functionality from scratch to monitor and validate outsourced administrator functions across over 50 funds, identifying over 200 discrepancies or errors in the first year.

Established institutional pricing discipline by founding and chairing the firm's first alternatives Valuation Committee, reviewing approximately 150 pricing issues per year across the platform.



Prism Capital Partners / Aerion USA Investment Management – New York, NY                                                               2002 – 2006

Global Head of Operational Risk Due Diligence and Limited Partner

Managed ~$2.5 billion allocated across 8 global hedge fund of funds portfolios comprised of ~70 managers across convertible arbitrage, fixed income, and event-driven strategies.

Protected portfolio integrity by performing comprehensive investment and operational due diligence on 70 managers annually, including review of accounting, risk, trading, and compliance functions, and preparing approval documents.

Enforced operational standards by exercising veto authority over approximately 20 managers who failed to meet minimum operational risk thresholds, preserving platform quality.

Streamlined back-office operations by consolidating and managing 6 third-party service provider relationships, standardizing client reporting, and reducing operational overhead across all eight portfolios.



Military Service

United States Army 	

Team Chief / Russian Linguist, AN-TSQ138 Trailblazer Intelligence Team

Delivered mission-critical intelligence support to the 1st Armored Division in Germany and Southwest Asia in support of Operations Desert Shield and Desert Storm, requiring a Top-Secret security clearance.

Earned multiple military honors, including the Bronze Star, two Army Commendation Medals, and two Good Conduct Medals in recognition of exemplary service and mission performance.



Education

Hoosier University, Bloomington, IN, Kelley School of Business - Bachelor of Science, Finance

", "passions": "I love sports and volunteer my time for my children's sports activities.  I love reading murder mysteries.  Life is all non-fiction, so I prefer fiction when I read.  My family is my true passion and I can speak about them with no preparation.  Public speaking is considered the most anxiety provoking activity by many.  In my view, this is only true if you don't know and/or not passionate about the topic.  I have spoken to large groups about my children for over 30 minutes with no notes.  ", "assessFile": "", "assessType": "Affintus", "lifeEvents": "I grew up in a southern Indiana in a small farm town.  Our house was less than 500 square feet and there were 7 of us living there.  We had no indoor plumbing or running water until I was a teenager.  All in all, I thought we had everything as no one knows they are poor.  I was an exceptional math student and athlete.  I was lucky to have exceptional high school teachers in that small town.  

I started college at Northcrest University in the Engineering school as a chemical engineer.  Although my grades were good, I transferred to Hoosier University to become a teacher and a coach.  My father convinced me that wasn't lucrative enough, so I ended up graduating in finance from the business school.  After a few years of working in a variety of low level management jobs, I enlisted in the US Army.

I served 8 years total between active and reserve.  I received a Bronze Star for my combat service in Operation Desert Storm.  This shaped much of my life beyond.  I had many traumatic experiences that have impacted me to this day.  

It wasn't until my 15 year high school reunion that I found a path to a real career.  An old classmate was starting a hedge fund.  I had no idea what that was, but it was the beginning of a 30 year career in alternative investments that lead me to Wall Street and Lyndhurst Brothers.  After the fall of Lyndhurst, my life shifted to finding a different path.  

At the end of the day, I still wish I would have become a math teacher and coach.", "resumeFile": "Martin Halverson resume.docx", "corrections": [], "linkedinFile": "Profile.pdf"}

## QUICK TAKEAWAY

You sit at the intersection of fashion retail operations and editorial storytelling, with 15 years of experience building brand experiences at store level and writing about culture for national outlets. Your strongest operational asset is building first-location retail presence from scratch: you opened and ran Vintage Pride's first out-of-market store for six years, which means you know how to translate a brand into a physical space where no one has heard of it yet. The distinctive piece: you have been both the operator (running the store, managing inventory, hiring staff) and the storyteller (writing for national music press, editing a 50-person college magazine), which gives you fluency in both the mechanics of retail and the narrative that makes people care.

## WHERE YOU SIT

You are a retail operations professional with 15 years of experience across fashion and lifestyle brands, ranging from small-format specialty retail (Vintage Pride, Pluto's Closet, Authentic Vintage) to large-format department stores (Nordstrom, Mountain Outfitters). You have managed P&L responsibility, hiring, scheduling, inventory, merchandising, and loss prevention across stores ranging from single-location independents to multi-department national chains. You have also worked as a contributing writer and editor for regional and national publications, including a nationally distributed music magazine and Cincinnati's weekly arts paper. You are currently operating your own vintage resale business while working in sales at Nordstrom. Your baseline is store-level leadership with full operational accountability, and your background includes both independent retail ownership and multi-location brand management.

## TRANSLATED ACCOMPLISHMENTS

*Built Vintage Pride's first out-of-market retail location from launch through six years of operations, establishing brand presence in a market with no existing awareness.*  
**Where this transfers:** Opening a first location in an unfamiliar market is the same operational challenge whether the brand is apparel, food service, experiential retail, or service-based franchising. You can build local awareness for a brand that does not have it yet, which maps to any retail expansion play, franchise territory launch, or brand entering a new geography where the customer base has to be built from zero.

*Supervised one-third of Mountain Outfitters' store business as Men's & Housewares Department Manager, overseeing two team leaders plus scheduling, loss prevention, and back-of-house operations.*  
**The operational move:** Managing a third of a store's revenue while coordinating cross-functional operations (sales floor, inventory, LP, scheduling) in a high-traffic urban retail environment. This scales directly to any multi-department retail operation, large-format specialty stores, or category management roles where you are accountable for a revenue segment within a larger P&L.

*Ran all store operations for Pluto's Closet as Assistant Manager, including scheduling, hiring, inventory, and social media at store level.*  
**Where this transfers:** Full store accountability in a resale model, where inventory is acquired rather than allocated, and margins depend on buying decisions at point of contact. This operational discipline transfers to any retail model with variable inventory (consignment, resale, vintage, pawn, liquidation) or any business where procurement happens locally and pricing has to protect margin in real time.

*Operated Authentic Vintage as owner since June 2020, managing all online business operations including product acquisition, listing creation, order fulfillment, and small part-time staff across photography, operations, and shipping.*  
**The operational move:** Running an end-to-end e-commerce business as a solo operator with contract support. You built the entire operational stack: sourcing, content creation, logistics, customer service, and team coordination. This maps to any small-business ownership role, marketplace seller operations, or early-stage e-commerce where one person has to build the entire machine before hiring a team to run it.

*Oversaw 50-plus student staff as Editor-in-Chief of Oxford Quarterly, running day-to-day magazine operations including content development and copy editing for all published work.*  
**Where this transfers:** Managing a large volunteer or student workforce where authority comes from influence rather than formal hierarchy. This leadership model transfers to any environment where you are coordinating contributors who do not report to you directly: freelance networks, volunteer organizations, community programs, or early-stage startups where formal structure has not been built yet.

*Wrote 1-2 articles per month for Subwave Music Press, a nationally distributed music magazine available at Barnes & Noble and FYE, from December 2009 to July 2012.*  
**The operational move:** Producing editorial content on deadline for a national publication with a defined editorial voice and distribution footprint. This discipline transfers to brand storytelling, content marketing, social media strategy, or any role where the business depends on consistent narrative output that connects with a specific audience.

## PATTERNS IN YOUR WORK HISTORY

**You have chosen small-format, high-touch retail over large-scale operations.**  
Your longest tenure was at Vintage Pride (six years), a specialty brand where the customer experience depends on storytelling and product knowledge, not transaction volume. Even your time at larger retailers (Mountain Outfitters, Nordstrom) was in departments where curation and customer relationship matter more than throughput. Patterns like this often signal a preference for environments where you can shape the experience at an individual level, rather than optimizing a system at scale. This orientation fits best in boutique retail, experiential brands, or early-stage concepts where the store experience is still being defined.

**You have built and run first-location retail, which often signals comfort with ambiguity and market-building capability.**  
Vintage Pride's first out-of-market store is the headline, but the pattern extends: you opened your own business (Authentic Vintage) during a pandemic, and your editorial work consistently placed you in early-stage or emerging contexts (college magazine editor-in-chief, intern at regional outlets before moving to national). This pattern often correlates with people who get energy from building something where the playbook does not exist yet, and who are comfortable with the risk that comes with being first. This capability transfers to any retail expansion role, franchise territory development, or brand launch where the market has to be built rather than inherited.

**You have moved between employee and owner roles, which suggests flexibility around structure and a willingness to trade stability for control.**  
You left a six-year run at Vintage Pride to start Authentic Vintage, then returned to W-2 work at Nordstrom while keeping the business running. This back-and-forth often signals someone who values autonomy but is pragmatic about when to take a paycheck and when to build equity. It also suggests you are comfortable with the operational complexity of running both at once, which is a specific skill set: managing your own time, protecting margin in your own business, and showing up fully in someone else's.

**Your career includes both retail operations and editorial storytelling, which is an unusual combination.**  
You have managed inventory and P&L, and you have also written for national publications and edited a 50-person magazine. These are typically separate skill sets, and the combination often signals someone who sees retail as a narrative exercise, not just a transactional one. This dual fluency shows up most clearly in brand-driven retail, where the story is the product (Vintage Pride, vintage resale, lifestyle brands). It transfers to any role where retail and content intersect: brand marketing, experiential retail, community-building, or retail concepts where the customer comes for the story as much as the product.

**You have stayed in fashion and lifestyle retail across your entire career, which suggests deep domain fluency and a specific cultural orientation.**  
Fifteen years in apparel and lifestyle brands, from fast fashion to vintage to premium department stores. This level of industry consistency often correlates with people who care about the category itself, not just the operational challenge. You likely have intuition about trends, product, and customer behavior that someone coming from outside the category would not have. This depth makes you immediately credible in any fashion retail context, and it also means your strongest opportunities will be in categories where that cultural fluency matters: apparel, accessories, home goods, or other lifestyle-driven retail where taste and trend awareness are part of the job.

If any of these patterns miss you, use the feedback box below and I will redo this section with the right framing.

## WMRANG & COMPASS

**You care about the story behind the product, not just the product itself.**  
Your editorial background (Substream, Oxford Quarterly, CityPulse) and your choice of retail environments (Vintage Pride, vintage resale) both point toward someone who sees products as cultural artifacts with context and meaning. This orientation often signals a preference for work where the narrative matters as much as the transaction, and where the customer relationship is built on shared taste or values rather than price or convenience. This fits best in brand-driven retail, experiential concepts, or categories where the product has a story the customer wants to be part of.

**You have built businesses and teams from scratch, which often signals a preference for creation over optimization.**  
Vintage Pride's first out-of-market store, your own vintage business, editor-in-chief of a 50-person magazine. These are all contexts where you built the structure rather than inheriting it. Patterns like this often correlate with people who get energy from starting things, who are comfortable with the ambiguity of early-stage work, and who prefer shaping the direction over executing someone else's playbook. This orientation fits best in roles where you are building the first version of something: new store concepts, brand launches, market expansion, or early-stage retail where the operating model is still being defined.

**You have stayed in customer-facing work across your entire career, which suggests relational energy and a preference for direct impact.**  
Even as a store manager or department manager, you were on the floor, interacting with customers, building relationships, and shaping the experience in real time. Patterns like this often signal someone who gets energy from people, who wants to see the impact of their work immediately, and who prefers environments where the feedback loop is short. This orientation fits best in roles where customer interaction is central, not delegated: boutique retail, experiential brands, or leadership roles where you are still close to the customer rather than removed by layers of hierarchy.

**Where to watch out:** Your background shows deep fluency in small-format, high-touch retail, which means you may be less comfortable in large-scale, process-driven environments where the customer experience is standardized and the manager's role is execution rather than curation. If a role requires optimizing an existing system at scale, or managing through multiple layers of hierarchy, that may feel like a poor fit. The watch-out is not capability, it is preference: you have built your career in contexts where you shape the experience, and roles that remove that autonomy may feel constraining.

## THREE PATHS FORWARD

### FAMILIAR GROUND

#### Same Role, Same Industry

**Store Manager or District Manager, Specialty Retail (Apparel, Lifestyle, Home Goods)**  
Your six-year run at Vintage Pride plus your Mountain Outfitters and Pluto's Closet experience positions you for store-level leadership or multi-store oversight at brand-driven specialty retailers. Target companies: Warby Parker, Allbirds, Parachute Home, Everlane, Rothy's, Bonobos, Outdoor Voices, or regional boutique chains expanding into Cincinnati or nearby markets. These brands care about storytelling and customer experience, not just transaction volume, which aligns with your operational orientation. You can also target emerging direct-to-consumer brands opening first physical retail locations, where your Vintage Pride experience (building a store in a market with no existing brand awareness) is a direct match.

**Retail Operations Manager, E-Commerce or Omnichannel Brand**  
Your Authentic Vintage business proves you can run end-to-end e-commerce operations, and your W-2 retail background gives you physical store fluency. This combination positions you for operations roles at digitally native brands building or scaling physical retail, or at traditional retailers building their online channel. Target companies: ThredUp, Poshmark, The RealReal, Depop, or any apparel brand managing both online and physical inventory. You can speak both languages, which is rare.

**Visual Merchandising Manager or Brand Experience Lead**  
Your editorial background plus your retail operations experience positions you for roles where storytelling and physical space intersect. Visual merchandising, brand experience, or in-store marketing roles at lifestyle brands that care about curation and narrative. Target companies: Anthropologie, Free People, West Elm, CB2, or premium department stores (Nordstrom, Saks) where visual storytelling drives the customer experience.

#### Similar Role, Different Industry

**Customer Experience Manager, Hospitality or Experiential Brands**  
Your retail operations capability (managing teams, building brand presence, creating high-touch customer experiences) transfers directly to hospitality, experiential entertainment, or service-based businesses where the customer relationship is the product. Target industries: boutique hotels, restaurant groups, fitness studios, experiential entertainment (escape rooms, immersive theater, event spaces), or membership-based communities. The operational moves are the same: hiring and training teams, managing scheduling and inventory, building a brand experience in a physical space, and creating customer loyalty through consistency and storytelling.

**Franchise Development or Territory Manager, Retail or Service Brands**  
Your experience opening Vintage Pride's first out-of-market store positions you for franchise development or territory management roles, where the job is helping franchisees or new locations succeed in markets where the brand is not yet established. Target industries: franchise-based retail (boutique fitness, pet services, food service, home services) or retail brands expanding into new geographies. You know how to build local awareness for a brand that does not have it yet, which is exactly what franchise development requires.

**Operations Manager, Resale or Circular Economy Platforms**  
Your Authentic Vintage business plus your Pluto's Closet experience gives you fluency in resale operations, where inventory is acquired rather than allocated and margins depend on buying decisions at point of contact. This operational discipline transfers to any business in the circular economy: consignment, resale, rental, refurbishment, or marketplace platforms where the business model depends on variable inventory and pricing discipline. Target companies: thredUP, The RealReal, Rent the Runway, Poshmark, or regional consignment chains. The operational challenge is the same across all of these: how do you acquire the right inventory, price it to protect margin, and move it before it becomes a liability.

### THE INDUSTRY INSIDER: Fashion & Lifestyle Retail Ecosystem

You have 15 years in fashion and lifestyle retail, which gives you fluency in the category and credibility across the ecosystem. The insider advantage is real: you understand how the industry thinks, what problems keep store operators up at night, and how decisions get made at store level. That fluency transfers across the full ecosystem.

**Vendor or Service Provider to Retail (Sales, Account Management, Customer Success)**  
Companies that sell to retail need people who have worked in retail and understand the operator's problems. Target companies: POS systems (Square, Shopify, Lightspeed), inventory management software (Cin7, Katana, Fishbowl), e-commerce platforms (Shopify Plus, BigCommerce), retail analytics (RetailNext, Edited, Stackline), or staffing agencies that place retail talent. Your retail operations background makes you credible in sales or customer success roles where the buyer is a store operator or retail brand. You can speak their language, understand their constraints, and position solutions in terms they care about.

**Retail Consultant or Fractional Operations Leader**  
Your track record (opening Vintage Pride's first location, running your own business, managing operations across multiple brands) positions you to advise early-stage retail brands or small operators who need operational discipline but cannot afford a full-time hire. Target clients: emerging DTC brands opening first physical locations, regional boutiques scaling to multiple stores, or franchise operators who need operational support. This could be independent consulting, fractional COO work, or advisory roles through retail consulting firms.

**Brand Partnerships or Business Development, Fashion or Lifestyle Platforms**  
Your editorial background plus your retail operations experience positions you for roles where storytelling and commercial relationships intersect. Target companies: fashion marketplaces (Farfetch, GOAT, StockX), content platforms (Refinery29, Who What Wear, The Zoe Report), or brand aggregators (Thrasio, Heyday, Perch) that need people who can identify emerging brands, build partnerships, and speak both the creative and commercial languages.

**Buying or Merchandising, Specialty Retail or Department Stores**  
Your retail operations experience gives you fluency in what sells and what does not, and your editorial background gives you taste and trend awareness. This combination positions you for buying or merchandising roles at specialty retailers or department stores, where the job is curating product assortments based on customer behavior and cultural trends. Target companies: Nordstrom, Bloomingdale's, or specialty chains (Anthropologie, Free People, Mountain Outfitters) where buying decisions depend on both data and intuition.

**Trade Association, Industry Organization, or Retail Education**  
The retail industry has trade associations, educational programs, and advocacy organizations that need people who have worked in the industry and can speak credibly to operators. Target organizations: National Retail Federation, Retail Industry Leaders Association, or regional retail associations. Roles could include member services, education and training, advocacy, or event programming. This path fits if you want to stay in the industry but step back from day-to-day operations.

### WORK THAT MATTERS (Ikigai)

This lane is built on the Ikigai framework: the intersection of what you love, what you are good at, what the world needs, and what you can be paid for. It is most applicable for people ready for more meaning in their work, or at a career stage where legacy matters more than maximizing compensation.

**Building Retail Concepts That Support Local Artisans or Makers**  
Your operational capability (opening stores, managing inventory, building brand presence) combined with your editorial storytelling background positions you to build retail concepts that give local makers, artisans, or small-batch producers a physical presence they could not afford on their own. This could take the form of a curated marketplace, a rotating vendor model, a co-op retail space, or a consignment-based gallery. The operational challenge is the same as what you have done before (managing inventory, hiring staff, building customer relationships), but the mission shifts: you are building economic opportunity for people who make things, not just selling product. This could be independent (you own it), fractional (you advise or operate it part-time), or W-2 (you run it for a nonprofit or community development organization).

**Retail Training or Workforce Development for Underserved Communities**  
Your 15 years of retail operations experience, including hiring and managing teams, positions you to teach retail skills to people who face barriers to employment: formerly incarcerated individuals, people experiencing homelessness, immigrants, or young people in underserved communities. This could take the form of teaching through a nonprofit workforce development program, running a social enterprise retail training program, or building a retail apprenticeship model inside an existing business. The operational moves are the same (training staff, managing inventory, building customer service capability), but the impact shifts: you are building economic mobility, not just running a store.

**Storytelling or Content Strategy for Mission-Driven Brands**  
Your editorial background (writing for national publications, editing a 50-person magazine) combined with your retail fluency positions you to build content strategies for brands where the story is the product: fair trade companies, sustainable fashion brands, B Corps, or social enterprises. This could be a W-2 role (Brand Storyteller, Content Lead, Editorial Director) or independent consulting work. The work itself is the same (writing, editing, shaping narrative), but the mission shifts: you are helping people understand why the product matters, not just what it does.

**Circular Economy or Sustainable Fashion Operations**  
Your vintage resale business plus your retail operations background positions you to build or scale businesses in the circular economy: resale, rental, refurbishment, upcycling, or take-back programs. This could be independent (starting your own sustainable fashion business), fractional (advising early-stage circular economy brands), or W-2 (operations roles at companies like thredUP, The RealReal, Rent the Runway, or brands building take-back or resale programs). The operational challenge is the same as what you have done before (managing variable inventory, protecting margin, building customer loyalty), but the impact shifts: you are reducing waste and extending product life cycles, not just moving inventory.

## QUICK TAKEAWAY

You care about authenticity in a way that shapes every context you choose: the products you sell (vintage pieces with history, not mass-produced trends), the way you show up with customers (genuine care, not transactional service), and the environments you stay in longest (Vintage Pride for six years, a brand built on storytelling and nostalgia). Your drive comes from being seen as sincere and attentive, which your reputation data confirms people feel immediately when they work with you. You have built a career at the intersection of curation and connection: you pick the right thing (product, story, experience) and you make people feel something when they encounter it. The tension worth naming: your assessment shows you prefer autonomy and minimal structure, but your work history shows sustained performance in W-2 retail roles with clear procedures and hierarchy. That gap suggests you have learned to operate inside structure when needed, even if it is not your natural preference.

## WHAT ENERGIZES YOU

**You care about things that carry meaning beyond their function.**

Vintage fashion, music, youth sports coaching, your own kids. These share a common thread: they connect people to something larger than the transaction. A vintage piece has a story. A concert is a shared experience. Coaching youth sports builds something in kids that lasts beyond the season. Your work history confirms this: you spent six years at Vintage Pride, a brand that sells nostalgia and cultural memory, not just apparel. You write about music for national publications. You run a vintage business where every item has provenance. The pattern suggests you get energy from work where the thing itself matters to the person buying it, not just what it does for them. Transactional retail would drain you. Curation and storytelling sustain you.

## HOW YOU SHOW UP

**People feel your care before you say anything.**

Your reputation data is consistent: genuineness, sincerity, attentiveness, thoughtfulness, level of care. These are not skills you perform; they are how you naturally operate. Your assessment shows high sociability and persuasiveness, which means you get energy from people and you enjoy influencing them, but the reputation signals clarify how that shows up: you do not push, you connect. Customers at Nordstrom compliment your personal style and your attention to detail. Colleagues call you in when a VIP walks in or when a customer is upset, because you make people feel seen. The combination of high persuasiveness and high sincerity is unusual: most people who enjoy influencing others do it through logic or charisma. You do it through care. The watch-out: your assessment shows reactiveness and lower self-reliance, which means your mood can be influenced by what is happening around you, and you may attribute outcomes to external factors more than to your own actions. In high-stress or chaotic environments, that tendency could drain you. You thrive when the environment is stable enough for you to focus on the people in front of you.

## WHAT FUELS YOUR DRIVE

**You sustain effort when the work connects to something you believe in.**

Your work history shows stamina: you ran Vintage Pride's first out-of-market store for six years, you have operated your own business since 2012 while holding down W-2 roles, you coach youth sports, you are raising two kids. Your assessment confirms high stamina and a preference for a quick work pace. But the pattern across your career suggests the fuel is not just volume or intensity. You stay when the work means something. Six years at Vintage Pride, the longest tenure on your resume, was a brand you cared about. You left to start your own business, which gave you control over what you sold and how you sold it. You returned to W-2 work at Nordstrom, a brand known for service culture and employee investment, not just a paycheck. The pattern suggests you can grind through hard work, but the grinding needs to be in service of something that matters to you. Work that feels purely transactional will not hold you. Work that connects to your values (authenticity, care, quality, storytelling) will pull sustained effort out of you even when it is hard.

## WHERE YOU'RE GROWING

**You are building business fluency while staying close to the customer.**

Your vintage business has taught you end-to-end operations: sourcing, pricing, listing, fulfillment, small team coordination. Your Nordstrom role is teaching you commissioned sales in a premium environment, where the customer relationship drives the revenue. These are both contexts where you are learning the mechanics of running a business (margin, inventory turn, customer acquisition) while staying in direct customer contact. The runway from here: you could scale your own business, you could move into store-level leadership at a brand you care about, or you could build or advise retail concepts where curation and customer experience are the competitive edge. Your assessment shows high openness to experience and a preference for creating solutions rather than following procedures, which suggests you would thrive in environments where the playbook is still being written. The scalability question is not capability, it is choice: do you want to lead at scale (district manager, multi-store operations), or do you want to stay close to the product and the customer (boutique ownership, fractional advising, brand partnerships)? Both are viable. Your growth depends on which one you choose.

## THE ENVIRONMENT THAT FITS YOU

You thrive in environments where the product has a story, the customer relationship is the asset, and the culture values care over speed. Small-format retail, boutique brands, or early-stage concepts where you can shape the experience rather than execute someone else's system. You need autonomy in how you work, even if you are operating inside a structure. You need to feel like the work matters, not just that it pays. High-turnover, transactional retail will drain you. Brands that care about their people and their product will sustain you. The ideal environment gives you customer contact, creative latitude, and a team culture where sincerity is valued, not dismissed as soft. You do not need a title to lead, but you need to feel like your care makes a difference.

Fifteen years in fashion retail, every choice points the same direction: Vintage Pride for six years (a brand that sells nostalgia and cultural memory), your own vintage business (every piece has provenance), writing about music for national outlets (shared cultural experience). Your reputation confirms what the work shows: people feel your sincerity before you say anything, and that genuineness is what makes customers trust you and colleagues call you in when it matters. **Your personal brand: You curate products and experiences that carry meaning beyond their function. ## THE GOLDEN THREAD

You choose work where authenticity is the competitive edge. Three things in your background point to this.

Your career shows it. Six years at Vintage Pride, the longest tenure on your resume, at a brand built on storytelling and nostalgia. These are places where sincerity is rare and valuable, and where your operational fluency in retail gives you credibility the moment you walk in.

## YOUR VALUE PROPOSITION

**You build first-location retail presence in markets where the brand has no existing awareness.**

You translated a brand into a physical space where no one had heard of it yet, which means you know how to build local awareness from zero. Your assessment shows high openness to experience and a preference for creating solutions rather than following procedures, which explains why you stayed six years in a role where the playbook was still being written. This capability transfers directly to any retail expansion play, franchise territory launch, or brand entering a new geography where the customer base has to be built rather than inherited.

**You manage full store operations while protecting margin in variable-inventory models.**

Your Pluto's Closet experience plus your own vintage business since 2012 gives you fluency in resale operations, where inventory is acquired rather than allocated and pricing decisions happen at point of contact. Your values show independence and freedom matter to you, which explains why you built your own business and kept it running even while holding down W-2 roles. The operational discipline this requires (sourcing, pricing to protect margin, moving inventory before it becomes a liability) maps to any business in the circular economy: consignment, resale, rental, refurbishment, or marketplace platforms where the business model depends on buying decisions that protect the bottom line.

**You turn customer service moments into brand loyalty through genuine care.**

Your reputation data is specific: colleagues call you in when a VIP walks in or when a customer is upset, because you make people feel seen. Your assessment shows high sociability and persuasiveness, which means you get energy from people and you enjoy influencing them, but the reputation signals clarify how that shows up: you connect through care, not through push. At Nordstrom, customers compliment your personal style and attention to detail. That combination (high persuasiveness plus high sincerity) is unusual, and it creates customer relationships that last beyond the transaction. This transfers to any high-touch retail environment, experiential brands, or service-based businesses where the customer experience is the product.

**You coordinate large teams where authority comes from influence rather than formal hierarchy.**

As Editor-in-Chief of Oxford Quarterly, you oversaw fifty-plus student staff, running day-to-day magazine operations including content development and copy editing. Your assessment shows high stamina and a preference for a quick work pace, which explains how you sustained that level of coordination. This leadership model transfers to any environment where you are managing contributors who do not report to you directly: freelance networks, volunteer organizations, community programs, or early-stage startups where formal structure has not been built yet.

**You produce narrative content that connects people to cultural experiences they care about.**

You wrote 1-2 articles per month for Subwave Music Press, a nationally distributed music magazine, for nearly three years. Your passions confirm music and concerts matter to you, which means the writing was not just a job. This discipline (producing editorial content on deadline for a national publication with a defined editorial voice) transfers to brand storytelling, content marketing, social media strategy, or any role where the business depends on consistent narrative output that connects with a specific audience.

## WHERE YOUR WMRANG AND YOUR WORK MEET

The overall picture shows strong alignment across most dimensions. Your function (retail operations plus editorial storytelling), your position in the value chain (customer-facing, building brand experiences at store level), and your pace (high-stamina, quick work tempo) all fit who you are. The tension worth naming: your industry has been consistent (fashion and lifestyle retail for fifteen years), but your mission alignment appears to vary across employers. Six years at Vintage Pride suggests the brand itself mattered to you. Your vintage business gives you full control over what you sell and how you sell it. The Nordstrom role may be more about the paycheck and the service culture than about the product category itself.

**Mission alignment seems to be the variable that predicts your tenure.** You stayed six years at Vintage Pride, a brand built on nostalgia and cultural memory. You left to start your own business, which gave you autonomy over curation and storytelling. You returned to W-2 work at a company known for investing in employees and service culture, but your passions (vintage fashion, music, youth sports) do not obviously connect to premium department store retail the way they connected to Vintage Pride or to your own business. This pattern often suggests someone who can operate inside structure when needed, but who sustains effort longest when the work connects to something they believe in. If that is accurate, the path forward likely involves choosing environments where the product or the mission resonates with your values (authenticity, independence, self-expression), not just where the operations fit your skill set.

**Your position in the value chain fits you well.** Fifteen years in customer-facing work, from store manager to department manager to commissioned sales. Your assessment shows high sociability, and your reputation confirms you get energy from people. You thrive when you are close to the customer, shaping the experience in real time, not removed by layers of hierarchy. If a role pulled you away from customer contact (district manager overseeing multiple locations, corporate merchandising, vendor sales), that might feel like a poor fit. If that does not match how you experience the work, the feedback box below will help us adjust.

**Your scale preference appears to lean toward small-format, high-touch environments.** Six years at Vintage Pride (specialty retail, single location), your own vintage business (solo operator with small part-time staff), even your time at larger retailers (Mountain Outfitters, Nordstrom) was in departments where curation and customer relationship matter more than throughput. This pattern often correlates with people who want to shape the experience at an individual level rather than optimize a system at scale. If you are considering district-level leadership or multi-store operations, the question worth sitting with: do you want to lead at scale, or do you want to stay close to the product and the customer?

The alignment picture suggests your strongest next step lives at the intersection of retail operations capability (which you have built across fifteen years) and mission resonance (which has been inconsistent across employers). The environments that will sustain you longest are the ones where the product carries meaning, the customer relationship is the asset, and the culture values care over speed.

Persuasiveness See detail
The degree to which a person enjoys influencing other people to take an action

How a person approaches work, the work pace and how the direct their energy

Power See detail
The degree of formality in the structure and decision making

Work Interactions See detail

The organization’s preference for a cooperative work environment

p1:
## QUICK TAKEAWAY

You have built a 20-year track record in B2B sales leadership, then pivoted fully into career coaching and executive transition work in 2021. Your biggest asset: you have sat in the seat your clients are trying to reach. You sold complex services into senior buyers at Fortune 500 companies, built verticals from scratch, and delivered eight-figure revenue growth across multiple firms. That credibility is what makes your coaching land. The distinctive piece: you are building Pathway Club as a media-enabled coaching practice, not just a service firm. Two podcasts, HBR advisory work, and content production are part of the business model, not decoration.

---

## WHERE YOU SIT

You are the founder and president of a career coaching and outplacement firm with B2B revenue, Fortune 500 clients, and a proprietary transition framework. Before that, you spent two decades in enterprise B2B sales leadership, carrying quota at firms like NRC Group, MRA, Numericon, and First Vantage. You have launched verticals, built sales teams, sold seven-figure contracts, and operated in high-velocity, high-complexity environments where the sale is consultative and the buyer is a C-suite executive. Your baseline is VP-level sales leadership in data, research, and business services. You have also built a board and advisory portfolio (Metro SHRM Chapter, Pro Scholas, Northridge Business Review) that positions you as a thought leader in HR and talent strategy.

---

## TRANSLATED ACCOMPLISHMENTS

*Scaled Pathway Club's executive coaching client base by 400% within two years through targeted digital outreach.*  
**Where this transfers:** You can build a repeatable client acquisition engine in any professional services context where the buyer is an individual (financial advisory, legal services, fractional leadership) or a company buying on behalf of individuals (outplacement, benefits platforms, corporate coaching). The operational move is the same: identify the high-intent signal, build the funnel, and convert at scale.

*Designed and launched a proprietary career transition framework that reduced average job search duration for clients by 35%.*  
**The operational move:** You productized a consulting process. You took tacit knowledge, codified it into a repeatable system, and measured the outcome. That works the same way in any advisory or consulting context where the value is currently locked in the consultant's head and the client wants proof it works. Coaching, change management, and implementation consulting all reward this move.

*Negotiated B2B outplacement contracts with three Fortune 500 companies, generating $1.2M in recurring annual revenue.*  
**Where this transfers:** You can sell a service where the economic buyer (HR, Talent, Benefits) is not the end user (the employee). That maps to any B2B2C sale: benefits platforms, employee assistance programs, learning and development providers, financial wellness tools. The sale requires translating individual outcomes into enterprise ROI, which you have done.

*Launched Numericon's Financial Services vertical, working with Buy Side and Sell Side firms.*  
**The operational move:** You built a vertical from zero in a firm that had no prior presence in that buyer segment. That requires identifying the unmet need, translating the existing product into the language of a new industry, and building credibility where none exists. This works the same way in any go-to-market expansion where the product exists but the buyer does not yet know it is for them. (Suggest quantifying: revenue at exit, number of clients signed, or deal size.)

*Delivered over $25MM in incremental revenue over four years at NRC Group and expanded the sales team to fully exploit market opportunities.*  
**Where this transfers:** You can scale a sales function in a high-complexity B2B environment where the sale is consultative and the team needs to be built, not just managed. This maps to any VP Sales or CRO role in professional services, data and analytics, or SaaS where the product is not transactional and the team is the engine.

*Architected Parker Martineting Research's sales effort, resulting in a 75% increase in annual revenue, and developed the strategy for launching the firm's most successful new product.*  
**The operational move:** You designed the sales motion and the product strategy in parallel. You were not just carrying a bag; you were shaping what the firm sold and how it sold it. That works in any senior sales leadership role where product-market fit is still being found and the sales leader has to influence the product roadmap, not just execute against it.

*Led the #1 region at Euler Hermes ACI by dollar volume and profitability in 1999, achieving 166% of New Business Objective.*  
**Where this transfers:** You can run a high-accountability, quota-driven sales organization and deliver. This is table stakes for any VP Sales or Regional Sales Director role in industries where the sale is transactional or semi-consultative (insurance, financial services, business services, technology).

---

## PATTERNS IN YOUR WORK HISTORY

**You have moved from selling complex services to teaching others how to sell themselves.**  
Your first 20 years were spent in B2B sales leadership, selling data, research, and business intelligence into Fortune 500 buyers. In 2021, you founded Pathway Club and shifted fully into coaching professionals through career transitions. The operational capability is the same: you are still teaching people how to position value, handle objections, and close. The buyer changed from a company to an individual, but the underlying work is the same. This pattern often signals someone who has internalized a capability so deeply that they can now teach it, and who finds meaning in the teaching itself, not just the doing.

**You have chosen high-stakes, high-velocity sales environments across your entire career.**  
From Xander Corp in the 1980s to NRC Group, MRA, Numericon, and First Vantage, you have consistently worked in quota-driven, complex B2B sales roles where the deal size is large, the sales cycle is long, and the buyer is senior. You have stayed in this lane across multiple industries (data, research, background screening, advertising technology). Patterns like this often correlate with comfort under pressure, a preference for performance-based compensation, and energy from the close itself. People who stay in high-velocity sales for two decades are wired for the hunt, not the harvest.

**You have built verticals and sales teams from scratch multiple times.**  
At Numericon, you launched the Financial Services vertical. At SecureSafe Technology, you opened the East Coast sales operation. At Parker Martineting Research, you architected the sales effort that drove 75% revenue growth. You have been the person who walks into a firm with no presence in a segment and builds it. This pattern often signals comfort with ambiguity, a preference for building over maintaining, and the ability to operate without a playbook. The operational move is the same across all three: identify the white space, translate the product into the language of a new buyer, and prove the model works.

**You have built a media and thought leadership layer on top of the coaching practice.**  
You co-host two podcasts (*Pathway Club Live* and *The Workforce Wire*), serve on the HBR Advisory Council, and held an Associate Editor role at *The HR Journal*. You produced content that became the most-read series in Q4 2024. You are not just coaching; you are building a platform. This pattern often signals someone who sees content and community as part of the business model, not just marketing. The operational capability here: you can build an audience and monetize it, which maps to any creator-led business, media-enabled service, or thought leadership platform.

**Your board and advisory roles cluster around workforce development and HR strategy.**  
Metro SHRM Chapter (Programming Chair), Pro Scholas (Advisory Board), Northridge Business Review (Advisory Council), and *The HR Journal* (Associate Editor). You are positioning yourself as a thought leader in talent and workforce strategy, not just a coach. This pattern often signals someone who is building authority in a domain, not just practicing in it. The roles compound: each one adds credibility that makes the next one easier to secure.

*If any of these patterns miss how you experience your work, the feedback box below will redo this section with the right framing.*

---
p2:
## QUICK TAKEAWAY

You operate from a deep conviction that people deserve honest feedback about what is true about them, especially when the stakes are high. The career pivot from B2B sales leadership to coaching was not a departure from what you do well—it was the same operational move applied to a different buyer. You spent two decades teaching companies how to see value they were missing; now you teach individuals how to position the value they already have. The force that integrates your choices: you care about people by refusing to let them settle for less than what they are capable of. That shows up in how you built sales teams, how you coach clients through transitions, and how you show up in every advisory role you hold.

---

## WHAT ENERGIZES YOU

**You are drawn to high-stakes moments where clarity matters and someone needs to hear the truth.**

The reputation data names it plainly: people call you when there is a crisis and they need someone who will act without hesitation. The emergency-call pattern (the Komodo dragon scenario, the eight lives saved) signals comfort with urgency and a preference for being the person who steps in when others freeze. Your work history confirms it: you have chosen quota-driven, high-velocity sales environments for two decades, and you built a coaching practice around the hardest moment in most professionals' careers—the one where their identity is on the line and the stakes feel existential. The passion inputs (juggling knives, jumping off waterfalls) suggest comfort with physical risk and a preference for activities where the margin for error is narrow. The through-line: you are energized by situations where the cost of getting it wrong is visible and real, and where most people would opt out.

---

## HOW YOU SHOW UP

**You lead by taking charge, and you bring people along by making them feel capable, not comfortable.**

Your Affintus assessment shows Leadership and Persuasiveness in the top range: you prefer work that allows you to take charge of groups, and you get satisfaction when others adopt your point of view. Your Sociableness score confirms you prefer to accomplish tasks through teamwork and interaction, not isolation. The reputation data triangulates: colleagues describe your superpower as reading minds, which suggests an intuitive sense of what people are thinking before they say it. The work-history pattern supports this: you have built sales teams, launched verticals, and scaled a coaching practice, all of which require enrolling people in a vision they did not see for themselves. The operational move across all of it: you make people believe they can do something they were not sure they could do, and then you hold them accountable to that belief. People experience you as direct, confident, and intensely present. You do not soften feedback to make it easier to hear.

---

## WHAT FUELS YOUR DRIVE

**You sustain effort when the outcome depends on you showing up, and when the person across the table is counting on you to deliver.**

The work-history pattern shows sustained intensity across two decades of quota-driven sales roles, where the scoreboard is public and the stakes are high. You delivered 166% of quota at Euler Hermes, $25MM in incremental revenue at NRC Group, and 400% client growth at Pathway Club. The reputation data confirms: people call you when they need someone with two arms and the willingness to act. The emergency-call framing is not metaphorical—it describes the actual role you play in other people's lives. Your Stamina score on Affintus is high: you prefer a work pace that is quick and requires more stamina than most others have, and you get bored if things are not happening fast. The fuel comes from being the person who does not quit when the situation gets hard, and from knowing that someone else's outcome depends on you staying in the fight. The Self Reliance score (moderate) suggests you believe results are usually a product of your choices and work, but sometimes you attribute them to luck. That tension is worth sitting with: the evidence suggests you produce results consistently enough that luck is not the primary variable.

---

## WHERE YOU'RE GROWING

**You are building authority as a thought leader in workforce development and talent strategy, and you are learning how to scale a coaching practice through media and content.**

The board and advisory roles (Metro SHRM Chapter, Pro Scholas, HBR Advisory Council) cluster around HR and workforce strategy, not sales. The podcast work (*Pathway Club Live*, *The Workforce Wire*) and the content production (most-read series in Q4 2024 at *The HR Journal*) signal a shift from doing the work to teaching others how to think about the work. The operational move: you are building a platform, not just a practice. The runway here is significant: you could scale Pathway Club into a media-enabled coaching firm with B2B and B2C revenue streams, or you could move into a senior advisory or executive role at a talent platform, outplacement firm, or HR technology company where your practitioner credibility and thought leadership both matter. The Openness to Experience score on Affintus (moderate-high) suggests you prefer work that requires you to create solutions and find the best path with moderate guidelines. The pattern across your career confirms: you have built verticals, launched practices, and designed frameworks multiple times. The next chapter likely involves building something larger than what you have built so far, or stepping into a role where your influence extends beyond the clients you coach directly.

---

## THE ENVIRONMENT THAT FITS YOU

**You thrive in performance-driven cultures where the scoreboard is visible, the stakes are high, and people are expected to own their results.**

Your Affintus profile shows high Stamina, high Persuasiveness, high Leadership, and moderate Reactiveness (your mood is sometimes affected by events around you, but not consistently). You prefer a quick work pace, you get bored when things are slow, and you are energized by being around people. The Competitive score (moderate-low) suggests you prefer a culture that generally promotes cooperation, with occasional competition between individuals. The work-history pattern confirms: you have stayed in quota-driven, high-velocity sales environments for two decades, and you built a coaching practice where client outcomes are measurable and the business model depends on repeat referrals and word-of-mouth. The environment that fits: one where results matter, where feedback is direct, where people are held accountable, and where the culture rewards effort and outcome equally. You do not need a lot of structure (your Conforming score is moderate), but you need clarity about what success looks like and the autonomy to figure out how to get there. You do not fit in cultures where consensus is the goal, where feedback is softened to protect feelings, or where the pace is steady and predictable. You need urgency, and you need to know that what you are doing matters to someone other than yourself.
